summary: This is a humorous meta-post where Gruber preemptively addresses reader objections to his previous essay 'The Art of the Parlay' about platform licensing and market share. Rather than engaging with each counterexample, he simply lists technologies and platforms that readers likely emailed him about — the Apple III, Lisa, OS/2, the Amiga, GEM, and others. The joke is in the absurdly long title and the deliberate refusal to discuss any of these items substantively. He also gets in a dig at GEM enthusiasts who overstate its resemblance to the Mac. The format itself is the argument: these historical footnotes don't undermine his thesis.
key_points: Gruber preemptively deflects predictable reader objections by listing them without rebuttal
The listed items are all failed or marginalized platforms from computing history that readers likely cited as counterexamples
The parenthetical on GEM reveals Gruber's actual knowledge depth — he's not ignorant of these, he's deliberately excluding them
The post's form (a bare list with a comically overwrought title) is itself the rhetorical argument
money_quotes: Things Unmentioned in the 'Art of the Parlay' Article, but of Which You Must Take My Word Were Left Out Because They Were Beside the Point, and Not Because I Was Unaware of Them
GEM (early '80s GUI OS for PCs from Digital Research; which was not nearly as 'Mac-like' as GEM aficionados would have you believe)
key_insight: The best way to handle predictable objections that miss your point is to acknowledge them without dignifying them with a response.
